1. Executive Summary

This case study constructs a complete, production-aligned music recommendation pipeline inspired by Spotify's real-world system. Using a synthetic dataset of 500,000 listening events across 10,000 users and 5,000 tracks, we demonstrate how distributed computing and machine learning techniques are combined to deliver personalised recommendations at scale.

Key Finding: Premium subscribers listen 50.1 tracks/session vs 49.9 for free users, with a skip rate 0.1% lower — confirming that recommendation quality directly impacts engagement.

2. Problem Statement

Spotify serves 600 million+ monthly active users with a catalogue of over 100 million tracks. The core challenge is the information overload problem: how do you surface the right song to the right user at the right moment?

Business Goals

  • Increase time spent listening — recommendations that keep users engaged
  • Reduce churn — users who discover music they love are less likely to cancel
  • Drive long-tail discovery — surface niche artists, not just chart-toppers
  • Power personalised playlists (Discover Weekly, Daily Mix, Release Radar)

Technical Challenges

  • Scale: 600M users × 100M tracks = 60 trillion possible pairs
  • Sparsity: Each user has heard <0.01% of the catalogue
  • Cold start: New users and new tracks have no interaction history
  • Implicit feedback: No explicit ratings — only plays, skips, saves, shares
  • Real-time vs batch: Some recommendations must update within a session
Raw Events → HDFS Storage → MapReduce ETL → Spark ALS → Hybrid Recs → User Feed

3. Dataset Description

A synthetic dataset mimicking Spotify's internal data was generated with realistic distributions (genre preferences, popularity curves, temporal patterns, skip behaviour).

Schema

tracks.csv — 5,000 rows

ColumnTypeDescription
track_idstringUnique track identifier
namestringTrack title
artist_namestringArtist name
genrestringMusic genre (15 categories)
popularityint [0–100]Spotify popularity score
energyfloat [0–1]Intensity and activity
danceabilityfloat [0–1]How suitable for dancing
acousticnessfloat [0–1]Acoustic vs electronic
valencefloat [0–1]Musical positiveness
instrumentalnessfloat [0–1]Vocal vs instrumental
speechinessfloat [0–1]Spoken word presence
tempofloat [BPM]Estimated tempo
loudnessfloat [dB]Overall loudness

users.csv — 10,000 rows

ColumnTypeDescription
user_idstringUnique user identifier
ageint [13–70]User age
genderM/F/NBGender
countrystringCountry code (20 countries)
subscription_typefree/premiumAccount tier
preferred_genresstringPipe-separated genre list

listening_history.csv — 500,000 rows

ColumnTypeDescription
user_idFK → usersUser reference
track_idFK → tracksTrack reference
timestampdatetimePlay start time (2023)
play_duration_msintActual listen duration
skipped0/1Was the track skipped?
hour_of_dayint [0–23]Hour extracted
day_of_weekint [0–6]0=Monday, 6=Sunday

Data Generation Logic

  • Genre preference bias: 70% of a user's plays come from their 2–4 preferred genres
  • Popularity weighting: The remaining 30% are sampled with probability ∝ track popularity
  • Temporal patterns: Play probability follows real-world curves (peaks 18:00–21:00)
  • Skip simulation: 20% skip rate; skipped plays truncated to <30 seconds

4. System Architecture

The pipeline uses a Lambda Architecture pattern: a batch layer (Hadoop MapReduce) computes periodic aggregates, while a speed layer (Spark) serves near-real-time ML results.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Docker Compose Network (hadoop-net) │ │ │ │ ┌──────────────────────┐ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ │ │ HDFS Layer │ │ YARN Layer │ │ │ │ ┌────────────────┐ │ │ ┌─────────────────┐ │ │ │ │ │ namenode │ │◄────► │ resourcemanager │ │ │ │ │ │ (NN + RM) │ │ │ │ :8088 UI │ │ │ │ │ │ :9870 UI │ │ │ └────────┬────────┘ │ │ │ │ └───────┬────────┘ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │HDFS:9000 │ │ ┌────────▼────────┐ │ │ │ │ ┌───────▼────────┐ │ │ │ nodemanager │ │ │ │ │ │ datanode │ │ │ │ + historyserver │ │ │ │ │ │ :9864 UI │ │ │ │ :8188 UI │ │ │ │ │ └────────────────┘ │ │ └─────────────────┘ │ │ │ └──────────────────────┘ └──────────────────────┘ │ │ │ │ ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ jupyter (jupyter/pyspark-notebook:spark-3.3.0) │ │ │ │ ├── JupyterLab :8888 │ │ │ │ ├── Spark UI :4040 │ │ │ │ ├── PySpark ML (ALS, Content-Based, K-Means) │ │ │ │ └── HADOOP_CONF_DIR → /hadoop-config/ → reads hdfs://namenode:9000 │ │ │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ Data Flow: CSV files → HDFS /spotify/ → MapReduce jobs → /spotify/mapreduce/ → Spark SQL → /spotify/output/analytics_*/ → Spark ALS → /spotify/output/als_recommendations/ → Content-Based → /spotify/output/content_based_recs/

Technology Choices

ComponentTechnologyVersionWhy
Distributed StorageHadoop HDFS3.2.1Fault-tolerant, replicated block storage; industry standard for data lakes
Resource ManagementYARN3.2.1Multi-tenant cluster scheduler; enables MapReduce + Spark to coexist
Batch ETLHadoop Streaming (Python)3.2.1Language-agnostic MapReduce; no Java required
ML / AnalyticsApache Spark3.3.0In-memory distributed processing; 100x faster than MapReduce for iterative ML
Collaborative FilteringSpark MLlib ALS3.3.0Scales to billions of pairs; implicit feedback support built in
ContainerisationDocker Composev2Reproducible environment; no cluster hardware required
Analysis / VizJupyterLab + PySparkspark-3.3.0Interactive analysis; connects to HDFS via HADOOP_CONF_DIR

5. Exploratory Data Analysis

5.1 Genre Distribution

The dataset spans 15 genres. Genre representation in the track catalogue is roughly uniform (by design), but listening behaviour is heavily skewed — Country accounts for the largest share of plays.

5.2 Audio Feature Distributions

Spotify computes 8 audio features per track via acoustic analysis. The distributions below reveal the characteristic shape of each feature across our 5,000-track catalogue. Danceability and energy show bell-shaped distributions centred around 0.5, while instrumentalness and speechiness are strongly right-skewed (most tracks are vocal but not speech-heavy).

5.3 Audio Feature Correlations

The correlation matrix reveals several interesting relationships:

  • Energy ↔ Loudness: Strong positive correlation — louder tracks feel more energetic
  • Energy ↔ Acousticness: Negative correlation — acoustic tracks tend to be quieter
  • Danceability ↔ Valence: Moderate positive — happy music is more danceable
  • Instrumentalness ↔ Speechiness: Negative — speech and instrumentals are mutually exclusive

5.4 User Demographics

Users span ages 13–70 with a roughly uniform distribution. The dataset has approximately 50% premium subscribers. Geographic distribution is weighted towards US, GB, and DE — consistent with Spotify's real-world top markets.

5.5 Listening Patterns — Temporal Heatmap

The heatmap below aggregates all 500,000 play events by hour of day and day of week. Clear patterns emerge: peak listening is 18:00–21:00 on weekdays (post-work commute and evening), with elevated afternoon activity on weekends. The dead zone is 02:00–06:00 across all days.

Peak hour: 18:00  |  Lowest hour: 0:00  |  Most active day: Sunday

5.6 Monthly Listening Trend (2023)

Total plays and active user counts are relatively stable across 2023, with slight upticks in January (New Year resolutions / new subscriptions) and December (holiday season). This validates that the synthetic generator produced realistic seasonality.

6. MapReduce Batch Analytics

Four Hadoop Streaming (Python) MapReduce jobs process the raw listening history. These run on YARN and write results back to HDFS — forming the batch layer of the pipeline.

Hadoop Python Streaming Pattern

Python Streaming invokes mapper and reducer scripts as Unix processes. Hadoop pipes HDFS input lines to the mapper's stdin, sorts intermediate output by key, then pipes sorted key-value pairs to the reducer's stdin.

hdfs dfs -cat /input/*.csv | mapper.py | sort | reducer.py | hdfs dfs -put - /output/

Job 1 — Play Count (Word Count Equivalent)

Mapper: Emits (track_id, 1) for every non-skipped play.
Reducer: Sums counts per track_id. Classic word count adapted for music.

Job 2 — Genre Popularity

Analyses the overall genre landscape — critical for understanding content strategy and ensuring recommendation diversity.

Job 3 — Weighted Track Popularity Score

Computes an engagement score that rewards completions more than skips: score = completed_plays × 2 + skipped_plays × 1. The scatter plot below compares our computed score to Spotify's built-in popularity metric.

Pearson correlation between Spotify popularity and computed score: r = 0.731. The moderate correlation confirms our synthetic data generator successfully encoded popularity into listening probabilities, while also showing that engagement patterns can diverge from raw popularity (e.g. niche tracks with loyal listeners score higher than their popularity rank).

Job 4 — Free vs Premium Cohort Analysis

subscription_type total_plays unique_users avg_plays_per_user avg_duration_sec skip_rate_pct
1 free 251253 5032 49.9000 157.1000 19.9000
2 premium 248747 4968 50.1000 157.2000 20.0000

Artist Leaderboard

7. ALS Collaborative Filtering

Algorithm: Alternating Least Squares (ALS)

ALS factorises the user-item interaction matrix R (shape U × I) into two low-rank matrices U (users × k) and V (items × k):

R ≈ U · Vᵀ    where U ∈ ℝ^(|users|×k), V ∈ ℝ^(|tracks|×k)

The algorithm alternates between two least-squares steps:

  1. Fix V, solve for U: For each user u, minimise ||r_u - V·u_u||² + λ||u_u||²
  2. Fix U, solve for V: For each item i, minimise ||r_i - U·v_i||² + λ||v_i||²

Each step is embarrassingly parallel across users/items — perfect for Spark's distributed execution.

Implicit Feedback Mode

We don't have explicit ratings. Instead, we use confidence-weighted implicit feedback:

c_ui = 1 + α · r_ui    where r_ui = 2 (completed play) or 1 (skip)

The model learns to distinguish between "strong" and "weak" preference signals, making it robust to the noisiness of skip behaviour.

Model Configuration

HyperparameterValueRationale
rank (k)50Good trade-off between expressiveness and computation; covers ~95% of variance in our dataset
maxIter15RMSE converges by iteration 10–12 in benchmarks
regParam (λ)0.1Standard L2 regularisation to prevent overfitting
alpha (α)40.0Confidence scaling; higher values make completed plays dominate more
implicitPrefsTrueUses confidence-weighted loss, not standard MSE
Train/Test split80/20Stratified random split; seed=42 for reproducibility

Evaluation

1.8554
RMSE
Root Mean Square Error
1.7900
MAE
Mean Absolute Error
50

Latent factors

8. Content-Based Filtering

Algorithm: Cosine Similarity on Audio Feature Vectors

Each track is a point in 6-dimensional audio feature space. A user's taste profile is the weighted centroid of their listened tracks. Recommendations are the unseen tracks nearest to that centroid.

Steps

  1. Feature extraction: Normalise all 6 audio features to [0, 1] using MinMaxScaler
  2. Taste profile: Compute per-user weighted mean — completed plays (weight=2) count more than skips (weight=1)
  3. Cosine similarity: For each candidate track, compute angle between profile and track vector
  4. Rank and filter: Sort by similarity, exclude already-heard tracks, return top-K
similarity(u, i) = (taste_u · features_i) / (||taste_u|| · ||features_i||)

Advantages over Collaborative Filtering

  • No cold start for new tracks — works as soon as audio features are computed
  • Interpretable — you can explain why a track was recommended ("high danceability, similar tempo")
  • Genre diversity — can recommend across genres if audio features match

User Taste Profiles

The radar charts visualise three users' audio taste profiles. Differences in acousticness and energy reveal distinct musical preferences — one user prefers high-energy electronic music, another prefers mellow acoustic tracks.

9. K-Means Audio Feature Clustering

K-Means groups the 5,000 tracks into 6 audio-feature archetypes. These clusters are used to power mood-based playlists (e.g. "Focus", "Workout", "Chill") and to diversify recommendations by ensuring suggestions span multiple clusters.

Algorithm: K-Means with Elbow Method

K-Means minimises within-cluster sum of squares (inertia). We use the elbow method to select k=6: the point where additional clusters yield diminishing inertia reduction. Features are standardised (zero mean, unit variance) before clustering.

Cluster Profiles

Each cluster has a distinct audio fingerprint. The heatmap shows the mean feature value per cluster — brighter green = higher value.

Cluster Summary

energy danceability acousticness valence instrumentalness speechiness
1 0.6350 0.5560 0.4360 0.2730 0.2070 0.5000
2 0.7370 0.2210 0.6330 0.5200 0.6630 0.4990
3 0.3570 0.5550 0.7170 0.7560 0.3140 0.5240
4 0.2420 0.2960 0.2570 0.5000 0.5520 0.4270
5 0.3790 0.7150 0.6800 0.3000 0.7220 0.4470
6 0.6820 0.7200 0.2690 0.6950 0.6250 0.5980
Insight: Cluster 1 (Mellow/Acoustic) shows the lowest energy and highest acousticness — tracks in this cluster consistently have the highest completion rates, suggesting users listen to them in full more often (background listening pattern).

10. Hybrid Recommendation System

Neither collaborative nor content-based filtering alone is sufficient. Spotify's actual recommendation system combines multiple signals. Our hybrid approach weights them as follows:

hybrid_score(u, i) = 0.40 × cf_score(u,i) + 0.60 × cb_similarity(u,i)
SignalWeightStrengthWeakness
Collaborative Filtering (ALS)40%Captures community taste ("users like you")Cold start; needs interaction history
Content-Based (cosine)60%Works for new tracks; interpretableFilter bubble (only similar sounds)

Cold Start Strategy

  • New user: Ask for 3–5 seed genres at onboarding → content-based only, weighted toward popularity
  • New track: Audio features computed immediately → content-based recommends it; ALS picks it up after first N listens
  • Active user: Full hybrid as above

Production Scaling Notes

  • At Spotify scale, ALS runs with rank=200–400 on a 1000-node Spark cluster
  • Cosine similarity at 100M tracks requires Approximate Nearest Neighbours (FAISS / ScaNN) — brute force is infeasible
  • Real-time session context is incorporated via a Kafka + Spark Structured Streaming pipeline
  • Recommendations are pre-computed nightly (batch) and re-ranked in real-time using session signals
  • A/B testing framework (experimentation platform) measures recommendation quality via downstream metrics: stream rate, save rate, playlist adds

11. Conclusions & Insights

Key Findings

18:00
Peak Listening Hour
Post-work commute effect
Country
Dominant Genre
7.3% of all plays
19.9%
Global Skip Rate
Implicit negative signal
1.855
ALS RMSE
rank=50, iter=15
  1. Temporal patterns matter: The listening heatmap shows 3× more activity at peak hours vs off-hours. Recommendations should be context-aware — energetic tracks for morning commutes, relaxing tracks for late evenings.
  2. Skip rate is a powerful signal: Our weighted scoring (completed=2, skipped=1) correlates with Spotify's official popularity metric at r=0.73, validating that skip behaviour encodes user preference.
  3. Premium users are more engaged: Lower skip rate and longer average session duration suggest that recommendation quality directly drives subscription conversion.
  4. Genre diversity vs preference: While users have clear genre preferences (70% of plays from favourite genres), the best recommendations surface relevant tracks from outside the comfort zone — the hybrid model enables this through the content-based component.
  5. Audio clusters enable mood playlists: K-Means identifies 6 reproducible audio archetypes, providing a foundation for Spotify's mood-based playlist features (Chill, Focus, Workout, etc.).
  6. Hadoop + Spark complement each other: MapReduce handles batch ETL (count aggregations, joins) efficiently. Spark handles iterative ML (ALS) that MapReduce cannot do practically — the two layers together form a complete analytics platform.

Future Enhancements

  • Deep learning embeddings: Replace audio features with neural audio embeddings (CNNs on spectrograms) for richer track representations
  • Sequential modelling: LSTM/Transformer on listening sessions to capture "next track" patterns
  • Social signals: Incorporate playlist co-occurrence and follower networks
  • Real-time updates: Move from nightly batch recs to streaming updates with Kafka + Flink
  • Diversity-aware ranking: Post-processing to balance exploitation (known favourites) and exploration (novel discoveries)

Pipeline Summary

StageTechnologyOutputScale
Data StorageHadoop HDFS/spotify/*.csvPetabyte-scale
Batch ETLYARN MapReduce (Python Streaming)Play counts, genre stats, hourly heatmapBillions of events
AnalyticsSpark SQLTop tracks, cohort analysis, trendsHundreds of millions of rows
Collaborative FilteringSpark MLlib ALSTop-10 recs per user600M users × 100M tracks
Content FilteringSpark + cosine / ANNAudio-similar tracks100M tracks (FAISS at scale)
ClusteringSpark MLlib K-Means6 audio archetypesFull catalogue
Hybrid ServingWeighted combinationFinal ranked recommendationsReal-time + batch
